title: Understanding the consumer unit
subtitle: What each device does and when to call an electrician
excerpt: A reference article explaining the domestic consumer unit (fuse box): the main switch, RCDs, MCBs, RCBOs, and fuses; what each device protects against; how to identify a tripped breaker; and a clear guide to what work is and is not legal to do yourself.
sourceNotes: IET Wiring Regulations 18th Edition (BS 7671). The Building Regulations Approved Document P. HSE guidance on domestic electrical safety. Which? Trusted Traders consumer guide to consumer units.

content.text: splits the electricity coming in from the meter and sends it out to the circuits of the house. It also holds the devices that protect people and cables from faults. Knowing what is inside the consumer unit is the first step for any domestic electrical work, including the simple faceplate swaps covered elsewhere.

The main switch is at one end of the consumer unit and isolates the entire house. Switching it off leaves the meter and the incoming cable live; do not touch those. The main switch is used when replacing the consumer unit itself (a job for a registered electrician only) or in a widespread fault.

trips when it detects a leakage of current to earth, which indicates a live conductor touching something it should not. A 30 mA RCD trips in about 40 milliseconds, which is fast enough to protect against a potentially lethal shock. In older consumer units, one or two large RCDs cover groups of circuits. In modern units, each circuit has its own RCBO.

trips on over-current, which means either overload (too many appliances) or a short circuit (live touching neutral or earth directly). MCBs are rated: a 6A MCB protects a lighting circuit, a 16A or 32A protects a socket ring. An MCB that trips repeatedly on a circuit that is not obviously overloaded indicates a wiring fault.

is a single module that provides both MCB and RCD protection for one circuit. A modern consumer unit uses RCBOs throughout so a fault on one circuit trips only that circuit's breaker, not a shared RCD that would also trip all other circuits on that side.

A tripped MCB or RCBO sits in a middle position between fully on and fully off. It must be pushed to the fully-off position before it can be reset to on. A tripped RCD has a test button that pops out slightly when tripped and must be pressed back in before the lever can be pushed to on.
What you can and cannot do yourself

of the Building Regulations in England and Wales, most domestic electrical work must be done by a registered scheme member or told to the local authority. Like-for-like swaps are fine without notice. That covers a socket faceplate, a light fitting, or a switch faceplate. The room must not be a bathroom or outside. Any work inside the consumer unit needs a registered electrician. So does any new circuit, any work in a bathroom or outdoors, or any work on the supply side of the consumer unit.

Every RCD and RCBO has a test button. Press it monthly to confirm the device trips; a device that does not trip when tested is faulty and must be replaced by a registered electrician. The test button is the only safe way to check a protective device; never defeat or bridge it.
